text: The surgeon begins the operation by dissecting the gastrohepatic ligament. The left gastric artery is visible overlying the operative field. The surgeon carefully skeletonizes the artery, places four clips for hemostatic control, and transects the artery between the clips using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon identifies the right crus and carefully sweeps to separate the pillar of the right crus from the esophagus. The dissection continues over the anterior esophagus towards the pillar of the left crus, avoiding posterior dissection to prevent exposure and potential injury to the posterior vagus nerve.

The surgeon exposes four to five centimeters of the esophagus below the diaphragm. A myotomy is performed over a six centimeter segment of the esophagus and a two to three centimeter segment of the gastric wall. The focus shifts to the mid body of the greater curve of the stomach, where the greater omentum and short gastric arteries are transected to mobilize the stomach fundus for fundoplication.
The surgeon divides the fat pad at the gastroesophageal junction, revealing the full dissection plane for the esophagocardiomyotomy procedure.

The surgeon begins the esophagocardiomyotomy at the eleven o'clock position on the esophagus, approximately three to four centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. Using the blade of the harmonic scalpel, the surgeon carefully dissects proximally along the esophagus, dividing the longitudinal and circular muscle fibers while preserving the deeper mucosal layer. As the cardia of the stomach is thicker, a hook cautery is used for distal dissection, pulling muscle fibers away from the mucosal layer to prevent thermal injuries. The circular muscle fibers become visible as they are further divided, and proximal fibers are transected with the harmonic scalpel. Near the gastroesophageal junction, the lower esophageal sphincter fibers adhere to the mucosal layer, requiring meticulous dissection with fine-tipped scissors.

The surgeon focuses on the GE junction, a critical area for esophagocardiomyotomy, where precise dissection of the lower esophageal sphincter is performed to ensure complete relief of achalasia symptoms.

The surgeon completes a full dissection of six centimeters of the esophagus and two to three centimeters of the gastric wall. An anterior partial fundoplication, known as door fundoplication, is performed using three sets of sutures. The first suture set attaches the fundus of the stomach to the left pillar of the cruci, incorporating the muscle layers of the esophagus. The subsequent sutures connect the fundus of the stomach to the mucosal layers of the esophagus.

The surgeon places the wrap in position and secures it with the second set of sutures, incorporating the fundus of the stomach and the rim of the esophageal hiatus to alleviate tension on the fundoplication. The final set of sutures is placed, involving the right pillar of the cruce, with each suture incorporating the fundus of the stomach and the right cruz.
